articles.title: Tiny silica particles wiped out aggressive prostate cancer in mice
articles.summary: Tiny silica nanoparticles engineered to seek out prostate cancer caused tumor cells to self-destruct and supercharged the immune system in preclinical mouse studies. Combined with immunotherapy, the treatment produced complete remissions in multiple mice, raising hopes for a powerful new approach to prostate cancer.
articles.content:
Researchers have developed tiny silica nanoparticles that can directly destroy prostate tumors while also awakening the body's immune system to fight cancer, according to a new preclinical study led by scientists at Weill Cornell Medicine and the Cornell Duffield College of Engineering. In mouse models of aggressive prostate cancer, the targeted particles produced several complete tumor remissions, offering encouraging evidence that the approach could eventually advance to human clinical trials.

Made from amorphous silica, a form of silicon dioxide found naturally in foods and the fossilized remains of microscopic organisms, the engineered nanoparticles appear to attack prostate cancer in multiple ways at once.

Tiny nanoparticles with a dual cancer fighting strategy

The nanoparticles, known as ultrasmall fluorescent core shell silica nanoparticles or Cornell Prime dots (C' dots), were originally created to improve medical imaging. They have already advanced into late stage clinical trials for image guided surgery and other therapeutic uses.

More recently, researchers discovered that the particles themselves can selectively damage cancer cells while leaving healthy cells largely unharmed.

In the new study, published June 15 in Cancer Research, a journal of the American Association for Cancer Research, the team tested the nanoparticles in mice with aggressive prostate cancer. They found that the particles made tumor cells highly vulnerable to a form of self destruction while also transforming the tumor environment from an immune resistant "cold" state into an immune active "hot" state. This shift could significantly improve the effectiveness of existing immunotherapies.

"We're very encouraged by these results; a treatment that directly induces tumor-cell death while transforming the immune microenvironment, as this does, would represent a new clinical paradigm," said senior author Dr. Michelle Bradbury, the Endowed Professor of Imaging Research in Radiology and director of the Molecular Imaging Innovations Institute at Weill Cornell Medicine and a neuroradiologist at NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medical Center.

The work is part of a long running collaboration between Dr. Bradbury's laboratory and the laboratory of co corresponding author Dr. Ulrich Wiesner, the Spencer T. Olin Professor in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering and a professor in the Department of Design Tech in the College of Architecture, Art, and Planning. The research received support in part from the Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy at Weill Cornell Medicine.

How the silica particles kill cancer cells

One of the most unusual findings involved a process called "ferroptosis," a specialized form of cell death driven by overwhelming oxidation inside cells. During ferroptosis, oxidation damages critical molecules, particularly the fatty molecules that make up cell membranes, eventually causing the cells to break down.

Scientists do not yet fully understand how the nanoparticles trigger this process. However, evidence suggests the particles, which were initially designed to carry imaging agents, can collect positively charged iron ions from the bloodstream and transport them into tumor cells. Once inside, those iron ions may fuel the intense oxidation that drives ferroptosis.

Reawakening the immune system

Beyond directly killing tumor cells, the nanoparticles also reshaped the immune environment surrounding the cancer.

The researchers observed that T cells, macrophages, and other immune cells near the tumors shifted from inactive or immune suppressing states into active cancer fighting cells. The nanoparticles also made tumors much more responsive to approved immunotherapy drugs. At the same time, they disrupted metabolic processes across several types of cells within the tumor microenvironment, further slowing tumor growth.

To ensure the treatment reached prostate cancer cells, the team attached a targeting molecule that recognizes PSMA, a protein found on the surface of prostate tumor cells. Although some particles briefly accumulated in other organs such as the spleen, the researchers found no signs of toxicity outside the tumors.

"It seems unreal -- how is it possible that rather than a single pathway we see all these effects happening simultaneously and only in tumors and not in healthy tissues?" Dr. Wiesner said. "I have to wonder whether ultrasmall silica's very early and ubiquitous presence in the environment and foods like leafy greens or cereal grains has given it a connection to biology that we're only beginning to glimpse."

Combination therapy produced the strongest results

The most dramatic findings came from survival studies involving mice with aggressive prostate cancer.

On their own, both the C' dots and immunotherapy modestly improved survival compared with no treatment. However, combining the nanoparticles with an immune checkpoint blockade therapy produced complete or nearly complete remissions and indefinite survival in four out of ten mice.

Adding a third treatment called CSF-1R blockade, which targets tumor associated macrophages, increased the number of complete remissions to five out of ten mice.

"We think there's nothing else out there that has such a strong and durable tumor growth suppressing effect," Dr. Bradbury said.

"One of the most intriguing aspects of this work is the convergence of direct tumor cell killing with broad immune remodeling," said study co author Dr. Jedd Wolchok, the Meyer Director of the Sandra and Edward Meyer Cancer Center, professor of medicine at Weill Cornell Medicine, director of the Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy at Weill Cornell Medicine Meyer Cancer Center and an oncologist at NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medical Center. "By creating conditions that support a more effective antitumor immune response, these particles may help unlock the full potential of immunotherapy in prostate cancer, where durable responses have historically been difficult to achieve."

Next step is human clinical trials

Dr. Bradbury also recognized the work of the study's co first authors, Drs. Nabil Siddiqui, Li Zhang, and Gabriel DeLeon, who led many of the biological, mechanistic, and translational studies, along with graduate students Nada Naguib and Rachel Lee from Dr. Wiesner's laboratory, whose careful synthesis and characterization of the nanoparticles were essential to the project.

"This study reflects years of collaborative effort across multiple laboratories and would not have been possible without the dedication, creativity and perseverance of this tremendous research team that helped drive the science forward," she said.

The research team is continuing to investigate these ultrasmall core shell silica particles as a potential new class of cancer therapies capable of influencing inflammatory, immune, and metabolic pathways at the same time. Their long term goal is to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of the treatment in human clinical trials.

Drs. Michelle Bradbury and Ulrich Wiesner are inventors on patents related to the technology described in this study.

The study was funded by the Department of Defense (PC220534); the National Cancer Institute, part of the National Institutes of Health, through grant numbers R01CA253658, R01CA243085, U54CA199081, the Cancer Center Support Grant (P30 CA008748), and Cycle for Survival/Parker Institute funding.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in prostate cancer treatment using engineered silica nanoparticles that not only directly kill tumor cells but also activate the immune system, leading to complete remissions in aggressive cancer mouse models. This preclinical success has broad implications for improving cancer therapy and is poised to advance to human clinical trials, representing a meaningful positive impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: New silica nanoparticles erase aggressive prostate tumors in mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Prostate cancer is a common and sometimes aggressive disease that can be hard to treat. Scientists have been searching for new ways to kill cancer cells and help the immune system fight tumors more effectively.

**What happened** – Researchers at Weill Cornell Medicine and Cornell Duffield College of Engineering created tiny silica nanoparticles called Cornell Prime dots (C’ dots). These particles were tested in mice with aggressive prostate cancer. The nanoparticles targeted tumor cells, causing them to self-destruct through a special process called ferroptosis. At the same time, the particles made the tumor environment activate the immune system. When combined with immunotherapy drugs, this treatment led to complete tumor disappearance in several mice.

**Impact** – This study shows a new way to fight prostate cancer by attacking tumors directly and turning “cold” tumors into “hot” ones that the immune system can fight better. The nanoparticles worked without harming healthy cells and improved the effect of existing cancer medicines. This combination approach is unique because it uses multiple methods at once to stop cancer growth.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to continue studying these nanoparticles to understand exactly how they work and to test their safety and effectiveness in human clinical trials. If successful, this could lead to new treatments for prostate cancer patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny silica nanoparticles combined with immunotherapy wiped out aggressive prostate tumors in mice by killing cancer cells and activating the immune system.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles nanoparticules de silice éliminent les tumeurs agressives de la prostate chez la souris
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer de la prostate est une maladie courante et parfois difficile à traiter. Les scientifiques cherchent de nouvelles méthodes pour détruire les cellules cancéreuses et aider le système immunitaire à mieux combattre les tumeurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Weill Cornell Medicine et du Cornell Duffield College of Engineering ont créé de minuscules nanoparticules de silice appelées Cornell Prime dots (C’ dots). Ces particules ont été testées sur des souris atteintes d’un cancer agressif de la prostate. Les nanoparticules ont ciblé les cellules tumorales, provoquant leur autodestruction par un processus spécial appelé ferroptose. En même temps, elles ont activé le système immunitaire autour de la tumeur. Associé à l’immunothérapie, ce traitement a permis la disparition complète des tumeurs chez plusieurs souris.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre une nouvelle façon de combattre le cancer de la prostate en attaquant directement les tumeurs et en transformant les tumeurs « froides » en tumeurs « chaudes » que le système immunitaire peut combattre plus efficacement. Les nanoparticules ont agi sans endommager les cellules saines et ont amélioré l’effet des médicaments anticancéreux existants. Cette approche combinée est unique car elle utilise plusieurs méthodes à la fois pour stopper la croissance du cancer.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit de continuer à étudier ces nanoparticules pour mieux comprendre leur fonctionnement et tester leur sécurité et efficacité dans des essais cliniques sur l’homme. Si cela fonctionne, cela pourrait aboutir à de nouveaux traitements pour les patients atteints du cancer de la prostate.

**Phrase clé** – De minuscules nanoparticules de silice associées à l’immunothérapie ont éliminé des tumeurs agressives de la prostate chez la souris en tuant les cellules cancéreuses et en activant le système immunitaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas nanopartículas de sílice eliminan tumores agresivos de próstata en ratones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer de próstata es una enfermedad común y a veces agresiva que puede ser difícil de tratar. Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de destruir las células cancerosas y ayudar al sistema inmunológico a combatir mejor los tumores.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Weill Cornell Medicine y Cornell Duffield College of Engineering crearon pequeñas nanopartículas de sílice llamadas Cornell Prime dots (C’ dots). Estas partículas se probaron en ratones con cáncer agresivo de próstata. Las nanopartículas atacaron las células tumorales, provocando su autodestrucción mediante un proceso especial llamado ferroptosis. Al mismo tiempo, activaron el sistema inmunológico alrededor del tumor. Combinadas con inmunoterapia, este tratamiento logró la desaparición completa de tumores en varios ratones.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra una nueva manera de combatir el cáncer de próstata atacando directamente los tumores y transformando tumores “fríos” en “calientes” que el sistema inmunológico puede combatir mejor. Las nanopartículas funcionaron sin dañar las células sanas y mejoraron el efecto de los medicamentos contra el cáncer existentes. Este enfoque combinado es único porque usa varios métodos a la vez para detener el crecimiento del cáncer.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea seguir estudiando estas nanopartículas para entender mejor cómo funcionan y probar su seguridad y eficacia en ensayos clínicos humanos. Si tiene éxito, podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para pacientes con cáncer de próstata.

**Frase clave** – Nanopartículas diminutas de sílice combinadas con inmunoterapia eliminaron tumores agresivos de próstata en ratones al matar células cancerosas y activar el sistema inmunológico.

articles.title: Lambs born via IVF using highly immature eggs in major breakthrough
articles.summary: Lambs have been born using an experimental form of IVF that coaxes immature eggs to become mature ones. This could boost the number of eggs available for fertilisation and improve IVF success rates
articles.content:
Lambs have been born using an experimental form of IVF that coaxes immature eggs to become mature ones. This could boost the number of eggs available for fertilisation and improve IVF success rates

Five sheep have been born via experimental IVF, one of which has produced two offspring of its own LAURENCE WINRAM

Healthy lambs have been born via an experimental form of in vitro fertilisation (IVF) that coaxes highly immature eggs to become mature ones in the lab. This is the first time the approach has been demonstrated in a large animal, raising hopes that it could boost the number of eggs available for the fertility treatment and improve IVF success rates. While further research is required, it may be of particular benefit when the ovaries have been damaged by cancer treatment.

“It’s really a major breakthrough,” says Stine Kristensen at Copenhagen University Hospital in Denmark, who wasn’t involved in the research.

More than 30 years ago, the same approach led to live births in mice. “Much more than mice, reproduction in sheep is quite similar to in the human body,” says Manjushree Boob at the Shubham Hi-Tech Hospital and Test Tube Baby Centre in Maharashtra, India, who also wasn’t involved.

Standard IVF involves daily injections for one to two weeks to stimulate immature eggs within follicles in the ovaries to develop into around 10 mature eggs. Of these, about six to eight are typically successfully fertilised in a lab dish. The resulting embryos are then implanted into the uterus, but this leads to a live birth in only around 20 per cent of cases.

That low success rate is why scientists are now looking at the potential of those immature eggs. Women are born with hundreds of thousands to more than 1 million of them, but the number declines throughout their life. During each menstrual cycle, one of these follicles forms a mature egg that is released during ovulation.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

In the latest study, Helen Picton at the University of Leeds, UK, and her colleagues collected dozens of these follicles from sheep. They were bathed in a cocktail of reproductive hormones – such as follicle-stimulating hormone, which stimulates their growth and maturation – and growth factors, which coaxed about 60 per cent of them to form into mature eggs.

They successfully fertilised around 30 per cent of these eggs to produce embryos, which were implanted into the uteruses of 18 sheep. This led to the birth of a female lamb in early 2024 and another four births this year. That female also had two lambs this year, Picton said while presenting the findings at the European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology conference in London on 7 July.

The results suggest the technique could boost the number of eggs available for fertilisation, which may improve conception rates, says Kristensen. But to maximise the chance of conception, this would probably be done in addition to standard IVF, so wouldn’t make it more convenient and could even add to the expense, she says.

Picton adds that the approach is unlikely to be used routinely, because retrieving the follicles involves taking a sample of the ovary lining, which is more invasive than collecting mature eggs during standard IVF.

But women whose ovaries have been damaged by cancer treatment could benefit, says Picton. Doctors often work to preserve their fertility by removing and freezing fragments of ovarian tissue beforehand, then transplant it back into the body when they are hoping to conceive, but there is always a risk the transplant contains cancer cells. Collecting immature eggs wouldn’t carry the same risk because cancer generally affects only the surrounding tissue, says Picton.

However, studies first need to explore whether human eggs grown via this approach can be fertilised. These studies are expected to take place in the next few years, says Kristensen. If they go smoothly, small trials can attempt to achieve healthy live births, but it could be five to 10 years before researchers gain ethical approval for this, she says. This is because such research must ensure the benefits outweigh any risks to the prospective parents or their children, says Kristensen.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in IVF technology demonstrated in a large animal model (sheep), showing the potential to significantly increase the number of eggs available for fertilization and improve IVF success rates. This advancement could have broad and meaningful benefits for human fertility treatments, especially for women with damaged ovaries due to cancer treatment. The article provides detailed context, experimental results, and future implications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Healthy lambs born using new IVF method that matures immature eggs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In vitro fertilisation (IVF) is a common treatment to help people have babies. It usually uses eggs that are already mature, but many eggs in the ovaries are still immature and not ready to be fertilised. Scientists want to find ways to mature these immature eggs in the lab to increase the number of eggs available for IVF.

**What happened** – Researchers in the UK successfully used a new IVF method on sheep. They took very immature eggs from sheep ovaries and used special hormones and growth factors to help these eggs mature in the lab. About 60% of these immature eggs became mature, and around 30% were fertilised to create embryos. These embryos were then implanted into female sheep, leading to the birth of five healthy lambs. One of these lambs has even had two babies of her own.

**Impact** – This is the first time such a method has worked in a large animal like sheep, which have reproductive systems similar to humans. The new technique could increase the number of eggs available for IVF, potentially improving the chances of having a baby. It might especially help women whose ovaries have been damaged by cancer treatment, as it uses eggs without the risk of bringing back cancer cells.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to study whether this method can work with human eggs. These studies will take a few years, and if successful, small clinical trials might start to see if it can lead to healthy babies. However, it could take 5 to 10 years before this method is approved for use in people because safety and ethical concerns must be carefully checked.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have shown that immature eggs can be matured and used for IVF in sheep, opening the door to improved fertility treatments for humans in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des agneaux en bonne santé nés grâce à une nouvelle méthode de FIV qui fait mûrir les ovules immatures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La fécondation in vitro (FIV) est un traitement courant pour aider à avoir des enfants. Elle utilise généralement des ovules déjà mûrs, mais de nombreux ovules dans les ovaires sont immatures et ne sont pas prêts à être fécondés. Les scientifiques cherchent à faire mûrir ces ovules immatures en laboratoire pour augmenter leur nombre disponible.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs au Royaume-Uni ont réussi à utiliser une nouvelle méthode de FIV sur des moutons. Ils ont prélevé des ovules très immatures dans les ovaires et utilisé des hormones et facteurs de croissance pour les faire mûrir en laboratoire. Environ 60 % de ces ovules immatures ont mûri et 30 % ont été fécondés pour créer des embryons. Ces embryons ont été implantés chez des brebis, donnant naissance à cinq agneaux en bonne santé. Un de ces agneaux a même eu deux petits.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’une telle méthode fonctionne sur un grand animal comme le mouton, dont la reproduction ressemble à celle des humains. Cette technique pourrait augmenter le nombre d’ovules disponibles pour la FIV et améliorer les chances d’avoir un bébé. Elle pourrait aider surtout les femmes dont les ovaires ont été endommagés par un cancer, car elle utilise des ovules sans risque de ramener des cellules cancéreuses.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier si cette méthode fonctionne avec des ovules humains. Ces études prendront quelques années et, si elles réussissent, des essais cliniques pourraient commencer pour voir si cela mène à des bébés en bonne santé. Mais il faudra peut-être 5 à 10 ans avant que cette méthode soit approuvée, car la sécurité et l’éthique doivent être vérifiées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont montré que des ovules immatures peuvent être mûris et utilisés pour la FIV chez les moutons, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs traitements de fertilité chez l’humain.
articles.personality_title_es: Corderos nacen sanos gracias a nuevo método de FIV que madura óvulos inmaduros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La fertilización in vitro (FIV) es un tratamiento común para ayudar a las personas a tener bebés. Normalmente se usan óvulos que ya están maduros, pero muchos óvulos en los ovarios están inmaduros y no listos para ser fertilizados. Los científicos buscan formas de madurar estos óvulos inmaduros en el laboratorio para aumentar la cantidad disponible.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Reino Unido usaron con éxito un nuevo método de FIV en ovejas. Tomaron óvulos muy inmaduros de los ovarios y usaron hormonas y factores de crecimiento para madurarlos en el laboratorio. Cerca del 60 % de esos óvulos inmaduros maduraron, y alrededor del 30 % fueron fertilizados para crear embriones. Estos embriones se implantaron en ovejas, dando lugar al nacimiento de cinco corderos sanos. Uno de estos corderos incluso tuvo dos crías propias.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que este método funciona en un animal grande como la oveja, que tiene un sistema reproductivo parecido al humano. La nueva técnica podría aumentar la cantidad de óvulos disponibles para la FIV, mejorando las posibilidades de tener un bebé. Podría ayudar especialmente a mujeres cuyos ovarios han sido dañados por tratamientos contra el cáncer, porque usa óvulos sin riesgo de reintroducir células cancerosas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar si este método funciona con óvulos humanos. Estos estudios tomarán algunos años y, si tienen éxito, podrían comenzar ensayos clínicos para ver si puede resultar en bebés sanos. Sin embargo, podría tomar entre 5 y 10 años para que el método sea aprobado para uso humano, debido a preocupaciones éticas y de seguridad.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos demostraron que óvulos inmaduros pueden madurar y usarse para FIV en ovejas, abriendo la posibilidad de mejores tratamientos de fertilidad para humanos en el futuro.

articles.title: Restoring dignity: Senegalese surgeon and Burundian foundation win reproductive health awards
articles.summary: A Senegalese surgeon who has dedicated his career to restoring the health and dignity of women living with obstetric fistula, and a Burundian foundation supporting vulnerable women and children, have been named the winners of the 2026 United Nations Population Award.
articles.content:
The UN's sexual and reproductive health agency, UNFPA, announced on Wednesday that the individual award will go to Professor Serigne Magueye Gueye of Senegal, while the institutional prize will be presented to the Fondation Bonne Action Umugiraneza of Burundi.

The annual award recognises outstanding contributions to breakthroughs which boost reproductive health and solve population growth-related challenges facing communities on the ground.

Tweet URL

For decades, Professor Gueye has treated women living with obstetric fistula, a devastating but largely preventable childbirth injury that can leave survivors incontinent, isolated and rejected by their families and communities.

Alongside performing life-changing surgery, he has trained surgeons from more than 45 countries across sub-Saharan Africa, helping expand access to specialist care throughout the region.

Commitment to dignity

Speaking to UN News, Professor Gueye said preventing new cases remains the greatest challenge. He called for stronger health systems and universal access to emergency obstetric care, including timely caesarean sections, saying these are essential if obstetric fistula is to be eliminated by 2030.

“Strengthening health systems in general, scaling up emergency obstetric care, to me is the key to eliminate fistula,” he said.

He also stressed that treatment does not end with surgery. Communities, he said, have a crucial role in identifying women who often remain hidden because of community stigma, supporting them through treatment and helping them rebuild their lives through social and economic reintegration.

Professor Gueye’s commitment extends beyond the operating theatre. To improve access to healthcare for underserved communities, he transformed his own home into the Aristide Mensah Health Center in Yeumbeul, on the outskirts of Dakar, and has long championed greater awareness of obstetric fistula across West Africa.

Reflecting on receiving the award, he described it as a recognition of the many people who had supported his work over the years rather than an individual achievement.

“I don't take it as personal recognition. To me, it is collective work,” he told UN News, adding that the honour encourages him to continue giving back to neglected and underserved communities.

He also had a message for young health professionals, urging them to develop their skills with humility and resilience while reaching beyond urban hospitals to serve remote populations where healthcare is often scarce.

Listen to the full interview here:

Soundcloud

At the ceremony at UN Headquarters, in a message delivered by UNFPA’s Deputy Executive Director Diene Keita, Secretary-General António Guterres said demographic shifts were reshaping the world and required a collective response, “particularly as they impact progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals and implementing the Pact for the Future”.

“To attain true demographic resilience, we must put the rights, dignity and well-being of all people at the heart of our development efforts,” he said. ‘This is not just a matter of policy, but also a moral imperative”, Mr. Guterres said in his message.

Congratulating the laureates, the Secretary-General praised Professor Gueye’s “clinical excellence and humanitarian dedication” and the Burundian foundation's work to improve maternal health and build local capacity.

Speaking at the ceremony, Professor Gueye said the award was the “recognition of the collective effort of making health the foundation of our societies”, accepting it “on behalf of all of those who think that a truly resilient population is a population that has health, autonomy and dignity”.

United Nations

Throughout his acceptance speech, Professor Gueye recounted the stories of women whose lives had been transformed through treatment, underscoring that every operation represents far more than a surgical procedure. "Behind each operation, there is a story," he said, adding that the true measure of success is giving women "back their lives" and offering patients in neglected and remote communities a second chance to regain their health, dignity and livelihoods.

Expanding access to health care in Burundi

The Fondation Bonne Action Umugiraneza works to expand access to healthcare, education and social support for vulnerable women, children and families across Burundi. In 2022, it opened the Polyclinique Umugiraneza in Gitega Province, bringing specialist services, including paediatrics, emergency care and fertility treatment, closer to rural communities. The foundation also supports care for women living with obstetric fistula, life-saving surgery for children with congenital conditions and services for survivors of gender-based violence.

Speaking at the ceremony, Burundi’s First Lady, Angeline Ndayishimiye, who leads the Bonne Action Umugiraneza Foundation, said that receiving this recognition from the United Nations was "both a privilege and a responsibility."

United Nations

"It inspires us to pursue our mission to even greater determination", she added.

Established by the UN General Assembly in 1981, the United Nations Population Award honours individuals and institutions for outstanding contributions to population and reproductive health. This year marks the 41st presentation of the award.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories

articles.personality_title_fr: Un chirurgien sénégalais et une fondation burundaise récompensés pour améliorer la santé des femmes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La fistule obstétricale est une blessure grave liée à l’accouchement qui touche de nombreuses femmes en Afrique. Elle provoque des problèmes de santé et l’isolement social. Son traitement nécessite une chirurgie spécialisée et des systèmes de santé solides.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Fonds des Nations Unies pour la population (UNFPA) a annoncé que le professeur Serigne Magueye Gueye du Sénégal et la Fondation Bonne Action Umugiraneza du Burundi ont reçu le Prix de la population des Nations Unies 2026. Le professeur Gueye soigne des femmes atteintes de fistule obstétricale depuis des décennies et forme des chirurgiens à travers l’Afrique. La fondation burundaise offre des soins de santé et du soutien aux femmes et enfants vulnérables, notamment en ouvrant une clinique spécialisée en zone rurale.

**Impact** – Ce prix met en lumière un travail important qui aide les femmes à retrouver santé et dignité. Les opérations du professeur Gueye réparent non seulement des blessures physiques, mais aident aussi les femmes à réintégrer leur communauté et à reconstruire leur vie. Les efforts de la fondation rendent les soins plus accessibles dans des zones souvent démunies. Leur travail montre comment la santé peut transformer des vies au-delà du simple traitement.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le professeur Gueye insiste sur la prévention des nouvelles fistules grâce à une meilleure prise en charge des urgences, comme les césariennes rapides. Les lauréats espèrent que leur travail encouragera un soutien accru aux systèmes de santé dans les zones rurales et défavorisées. Ils invitent aussi les jeunes professionnels de santé à servir hors des grandes villes.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Un chirurgien sénégalais et une fondation burundaise ont reçu des prix des Nations Unies pour leur travail qui change la vie des femmes en traitant les blessures liées à l’accouchement et en élargissant l’accès aux soins en Afrique.

articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La fístula obstétrica es una lesión grave causada por el parto que afecta a muchas mujeres en África. Puede causar problemas de salud y aislamiento social. Su tratamiento requiere cirugía especializada y sistemas de salud fuertes.

**Qué pasó** – El Fondo de Población de las Naciones Unidas (UNFPA) anunció que el profesor Serigne Magueye Gueye de Senegal y la Fundación Bonne Action Umugiraneza de Burundi ganaron el Premio de Población de la ONU 2026. El profesor Gueye ha dedicado décadas a tratar mujeres con fístula obstétrica y a formar cirujanos en África. La fundación burundesa trabaja para ofrecer atención médica y apoyo a mujeres y niños vulnerables, incluyendo la apertura de una clínica especializada en zonas rurales.

**Impacto** – Este premio reconoce un trabajo importante que ayuda a las mujeres a recuperar su salud y dignidad. Las cirugías del profesor Gueye no solo reparan lesiones físicas, sino que también ayudan a las mujeres a reintegrarse en sus comunidades y reconstruir sus vidas. Los esfuerzos de la fundación hacen que la atención médica sea más accesible en lugares donde a menudo faltan servicios. Su trabajo demuestra cómo la salud puede cambiar vidas más allá de curar enfermedades.

**Próximos pasos** – El profesor Gueye destaca la necesidad de prevenir nuevos casos de fístula mejorando la atención de emergencias, como las cesáreas oportunas. Los ganadores esperan que su trabajo inspire más apoyo a los sistemas de salud en áreas rurales y desfavorecidas. También animan a los jóvenes profesionales de la salud a trabajar fuera de las grandes ciudades.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un cirujano senegalés y una fundación burundesa recibieron premios de la ONU por su trabajo que transforma vidas al tratar lesiones de parto y ampliar el acceso a la salud para mujeres vulnerables en África.
